text: By the end of my first year I will have run a full cultural diagnostic and rolled out the new onboarding experience.
He had rolled out the framework before he had the buy-in, so it never gained momentum.
I would rather build on the groundwork than start over, and you had better hear the objection now, not after we launch.
I wish the objections came sooner, but they do not, so I read the room for the understatement instead of waiting for a direct no.
If I had prescribed a Brazilian answer on day one, I would have lost the room before I earned it.
What I am changing is the sequence, not the destination. It is the order of operations that makes the difference.
I am not fully sure how I would measure that yet, but here is the thing I would want to be true.
Alright, everyone's here. Danielle, it's your meeting, take it away. Tell the room, in your own words, what you were actually brought here to do.

Thank you, Grant. Let me frame my mandate plainly. I was brought in to lead the cultural transformation of the Canadian division, to run the diagnostic, close the trust gaps it surfaces, and build the People and Culture function the way I built it in Brazil. By the end of my first year, I will have run a full cultural diagnostic and rolled out a new onboarding experience.
Good. Now, a fair question from this side of the table: how is this different from what your predecessor tried and dropped?

He laid real groundwork, and I would rather build on it than start over. What I am changing is the sequence: he rolled out the framework before he had the buy-in, so it never gained momentum. I am running the listening tour first, earning credibility, and only then moving the needle, same destination, a different order of operations.
Here's the one I promised you wouldn't see coming. Forget the company for a second. What does success look like for you, personally, in this role?

Honestly, let me think on that out loud. Success, for me, is the day a leader in this room disagrees with me directly, in a meeting, because the psychological safety is real enough that they can. I am not fully sure yet how I would measure it, but if I have built a culture where people push back on me the way you just did, that is the whole job.
That's the answer. Not the polished one, the true one. Welcome to the team, Danielle. I think you're going to do just fine here.
Let me frame my mandate: I was brought in to lead the cultural transformation of this division, and by year one I will have run a full diagnostic.
What I am changing is the sequence, not the destination. I would rather build on the groundwork than start over.
I take the point about cultural distance, and where we may differ is on transfer. The method is neutral, even if the findings will not be.
I'm reading ambitious as a gentle way of saying you're not yet convinced. Tell me if I have that wrong, because I'd rather hear the real objection now.
Let me think on that out loud. I don't have a complete answer yet, and here is how I would find one.

Danielle, it is Grant Whitfield, VP of the Canadian division. I am leaving this the week before you fly in, because I wanted you to hear the shape of the meeting from me and not from a calendar invite. On your first Thursday, you are opening the leadership session. It will be me, the academic lead, and two of our regional directors, and I will be honest with you, everyone in that room has read your Brazil numbers and they are impressed, and everyone in that room is also quietly wondering whether the person who did all that in Portuguese can do it here, in English, in front of us. That is the real test, and pretending otherwise would not help you. So here is what I am going to ask of you. Open the meeting yourself. Frame your mandate, tell us what you were actually brought here to do, and do not undersell it. Then walk us through your People and Culture plan for the Canadian division, ten minutes, no slides you hide behind, just you and the thinking. And at the end I am going to ask you two questions you will not have prepared for, because that is where I find out who somebody really is. One of them will probably be uncomfortable. I want you to know that is by design, and it is not hostility, it is interest. The team you built in Brazil did not follow you because your grammar was perfect. They followed you because you saw them clearly and you told them the truth. Bring that person into the room on Thursday. The English is just the vehicle. See you soon.

Danielle, it is Ruth, your coach, and this is the last note I am going to leave you before Thursday, so I want to use it carefully. You have spent fifteen weeks building something, and I need you to take stock of it before you walk in, because you are the kind of person who will remember every word you got wrong and none of the ground you covered. So let me hold up the mirror for a second. Fifteen lessons ago you told me your fear was walking into that room and sounding junior when, in Portuguese, you are anything but. Listen to yourself now. You frame a mandate without apologising for it. You disagree with a doctor and hold your ground without losing the room. You give a struggling person developmental feedback that grows them instead of shrinking them. You read an understatement across a cultural gap that used to be invisible to you. That is not someone hoping to keep up. That is a leader. Here is the only instruction I have for Thursday, and it is not about tenses. When Grant asks you the uncomfortable question, do not reach for the perfect sentence. Reach for the true one. Hedge where you are genuinely unsure, that is honesty, not weakness, and then commit to the part you do know. The most senior thing in any language is not fluency. It is a person who can say I do not have a complete answer yet, and here is how I would find one. You already do that in Portuguese. On Thursday you are just going to do it in English. Go and lead the room. I will be listening for the story afterwards.
